Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 90 
By documenting these conditions, the Panji system sought to prevent the 
transmission of hereditary disorders, demonstrating an early, practical 
understanding of genetic inheritance. 
Archival Systems: Seven Types of Panji and Preservation Rituals 
To manage these complex records, the Panji system was organized into seven 
distinct types of registers, each designed for a specific genealogical or 
administrative function : 
│ 
┌───────────┬───────────┬────────┼─────────
──┬───────────┬───────────┐ 
▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ 
[Mula] [Gotra] [Patra] [Uteadh] [Aswajanam] 
These structural registries are detailed below : 
Registry Type | Specific Administrative and Genealogical Function 
1. Mula Panji | The original baseline register, recording the primary 
ancestral villages (Mulas) and Biji Purush lineages established in $1327\text{ 
AD}$. 
2. Sakha Panji | The branch registries introduced under Maharaja Madhav 
Singh, tracking social classes (Shrotriya, Yogya, Panjibaddha). 
3. Gotra Panji | Records the twenty patrilineal clans and their associated 
original ancestral villages. 
4. Patra Panji | Introduced around $1500\text{ AD}$ to track family 
migrations from original settlements (Mulas) to new villages (Mulgrams). 
5. Dushana Panji | Records lineage blemishes, non-traditional marriages, and 
hereditary physical or mental disorders. 
6. Uteadh Panji | A specialized tracking tool used to calculate Sapinda 
avoidance up to six generations before marriage. 
7. Aswajanam Patra | The registry of official non-relation certificates 
(Siddhanta Patra) issued to authorize marriages. 
A unique historical exception is found in the Rasadh-Araria registry, which is 
the only traditional Panji volume that explicitly records the personal names 
of women. In all other regional registries, women are recorded only by their 
maternal and paternal lineage connections.

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 91 
Because these documents were written on fragile organic materials, such as 
palm leaves and bark paper, the Panjikars followed a strict preservation 
routine. This routine was codified in a traditional Sanskrit mnemonic : 
$$\text{\textit{“Jalat 
Rakshet 
Tailat 
Rakshet 
Rakshet 
Shthila 
Bandhanat”}}$$ 
This instruction commanded the registrars to protect the manuscripts from 
water, oil, and loose binding. Additionally, the registries were dried once a 
year under the hot sun during the transit of the Sun through the zodiac sign of 
Leo (Simha Rashi, corresponding to mid-August to mid-September), when 
solar radiation is strongest, to prevent mold and insect infestation. 
Academic Traditions and Dynastic Inscriptions 
Mithila's genealogical codification was supported by a strong scholarly 
tradition led by the region's intellectual elite, known as the Shadayantri (six-
system) scholars. These scholars were master commentators on the six 
classical schools of Indian philosophy. 
The Panji records preserve the names of thirteen key Shadayantri scholars, 
documenting their ancestral lineages and contributions to the region's 
intellectual history : 
• 
Mahamahopadhyaya Godhi Sharma 
• 
Mahamahopadhyaya Madhav Sharma 
• 
Mahamahopadhyaya Adiya Madhav Sharma 
• 
Mahamahopadhyaya Shankar Sharma 
• 
Mahamahopadhyaya Yajnapati Sharma 
• 
Mahamahopadhyaya Parashurama Sharma 
• 
Mahamahopadhyaya Vamadeva Sharma 
• 
Mahamahopadhyaya Pitambara Sharma Vidyanidhi 
• 
Mahamahopadhyaya Gokulnath Sharma Phanandah 
• 
Mahamahopadhyaya Jagnnātha Sharma Phanandah 
• 
Mahamahopadhyaya Vamshadhara Sharma Rajanpura Darihara 
• 
Mahamahopadhyaya Raghunath Sharma Phanandah 
• 
Upadhyaya Datta (alias Vishnudatta Sharma Paramanandapuri) 
The Panji system also recorded the lineages of local ruling dynasties, 
providing valuable historical context for researchers. A notable example is 
the $17\text{th}$-century Sanskrit inscription from the Vashaiti (Araria)

This exhaustive classification reflects the structured approach to social

Modern Preservation and Genetic Validation

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 93 
In the modern era, the Panji Prabandha faces challenges from urbanization, 
changing social values, and the physical decay of historical manuscripts. As 
families increasingly rely on modern matchmaking methods, the traditional 
Saurath Sabha gathering in Madhubani has declined in participation, 
transitioning into a largely symbolic cultural event. 
To protect this historical archive, scholars launched a major preservation 
project. Led by author and paleographer Gajendra Thakur, Nagendra Kumar 
Jha, and Pandit Vidyanand Jha, the project digitized and transliterated over 
$11,000$ historical palm-leaf manuscripts from the traditional Tirhuta 
(Mithilakshar) script into modern Devanagari. This work was published as 
Genome Mapping – 450 AD to 2009 AD: Mithilak Panji Prabandh, ensuring 
the preservation of these genealogical records. 
Concurrently, research in genetic anthropology has begun to explore the 
biological basis of these traditional exogamy rules. Genetic analyses using 
single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) markers and Y-chromosomal short 
tandem repeats (Y-STR) indicate a strong correlation within patrilineal 
Gotras. 
These findings suggest that the strict exogamy rules maintained by the Panji 
system for over seven centuries have effectively limited the risk of 
inbreeding and preserved genetic diversity within these endogamous 
communities. The integration of these ancient genealogical records with 
modern digital archiving and genetic science ensures that the Panji 
Prabandha remains a valuable resource for studying human population 
genetics and social history.

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 94 
 
Chapter 6 
The Mithila Panji Prabandha: An Anthropological, 
Legal, and Socio-Historical Analysis 
The Mithila Panji Prabandha: An Anthropological, Legal, and 
Socio-Historical Analysis of India’s Written Genealogical Registry 
Historical Genesis and the Politics of Purity 
The cultural zone of Mithila, bounded geographically by the Himalayas to 
the north, the Ganges to the south, the Gandaka to the west, and the Kaushiki 
or Kosi river to the east, has historically served as a fertile crucible for 
intense legal, philosophical, and social developments. Within this region, the 
transition from decentralized, oral lineage introductions to a centralized, 
state-mandated written registry was precipitated by a crisis of social 
classification in Shaka 1248, corresponding to 1326/1327 CE, under the 
reign of King Harisimhadeva of the Karnat dynasty. Prior to this period, 
families sporadically relied on localized, memory-based introductions to 
verify bloodlines during marital negotiations. 
The institutionalization of the Panji Prabandha arose from a controversial 
court trial in the village of Gangaura involving Harinatha Upadhyaya, a 
prominent court pundit. Harinatha’s wife was accused of having an improper 
relationship with an outcaste of the Dusadh community. To demonstrate her 
chastity, she underwent a traditional fire ordeal, declaring "nāhaṃ 
cāṇḍālagāminī" ("I have not associated with an outcaste"). However, the 
ordeal resulted in her hands being burned, which the community interpreted 
as a divine confirmation of guilt. 
Distressed by this outcome, she sought counsel from Lakkhima Devi, an 
exceptionally learned female scholar and the daughter of the Mimamsa 
scholar Rati Mishra and wife of the legal authority Mahamahopadhyay 
Rameshwar. Lakkhima Devi analyzed the situation and discovered a

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 95 
structural infraction rather than a moral one: Harinatha had unknowingly 
married a woman related to him within the prohibited fifth degree of 
consanguinity, a direct violation of the exogamous marriage codes prescribed 
in the Smritis. Under Hindu jurisprudence, this transgressive marriage had 
technically rendered Harinatha an outcaste (cāṇḍāla). Armed with this 
realization, the wife demanded a retrial, altering her oath to declare "nāhaṃ 
svapativy-atiricta cāṇḍālagāminī" ("I have not associated with any outcaste 
except my husband"). This time, she emerged from the fire ordeal unscathed, 
proving both her personal chastity and the underlying structural corruption of 
the community's marital alliances. 
The realization that even elite scholars were entering into incestuous 
alliances due to the decay of lineage tracking deeply alarmed King 
Harisimhadeva. To protect the ritual purity of the priestly and administrative 
classes, the king ordered a comprehensive census of all Brahmin, Kayastha, 
and Kshatriya lineages in his realm. He convened a grand assembly known as 
the Vishwachakra Sammelan at the village of Jamsam, located in Pandaul in 
the Madhubani district, to formalize the recording of family histories. He 
appointed official registrars, known as Panjikars, led by Gunakar Jha for the 
Maithil Brahmins, Shankardatta for the Karna Kayasthas, and Vijayadatta for 
the Kshatriyas. While the Kshatriya lineages eventually drifted away from 
this system due to marriages contracted outside the territorial boundaries of 
Mithila, the Maithil Brahmins and Karna Kayasthas maintained the registry 
with mathematical precision for over seven centuries. 
This historical apparatus is preserved and evaluated in modern scholarship, 
notably in the volume Genealogical Mapping (450 A.D. to 2009 A.D.) - 
Mithila's Panji Prabandh, Volume II, edited and compiled by Gajendra 
Thakur, Nagendra Kumar Jha, and Panjikar Vidyanand Jha, and published by 
Shruti Prakashan in Delhi. This work represents a continuation of the first 
volume, which was titled Genome Mapping and consisted of three sections 
focusing on family history as the foundational phase of genealogical tracing. 
The Structural Typology of the Panji System 
The architectural foundation of the Panji Prabandha relies on the systematic 
categorization of individuals based on their paternal and maternal lineages. A 
Maithil Brahmin's identity within this framework is defined by several nested 
concepts: the Gotra, which is the maximal patrilineal clan tracing descent to 
an ancient Rishi; the Biji Purush, who is the earliest historically verifiable

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 96 
patrilineal ancestor; the Mool, representing the ancestral village associated 
with the Biji Purush; the Moolgram, representing a secondary branch 
associated with subsequent migrations; and the Shakha, which tracks the 
specific lineage of descendants down to contemporary generations. 
To mathematically illustrate the generational calculations used by the 
Panjikars to align historical figures within this framework, the generational 
interval can be standardized. The system calculates historical chronology 
using an estimated average generational span of forty years per generation: 
where represents the calculated historical epoch, represents the cumulative 
number of generations recorded in the lineage tree, and is the established 
generational constant of approximately 40 years. 
Through this formula, the Panji records have been used to verify the 
chronologies of several prominent figures of Mithila. For example, the 
lineage of the mathematician-astronomer Aryabhata (476–550 CE) is 
preserved within the Mandar-Kashyap Gotra, where he is recorded as the 
39th generation. Similarly, the legendary philosopher and humorist Gonu Jha 
is placed in the Vatsa Gotra (Karamhe Sonkariyam branch) at the 24th 
generation, establishing his birth in approximately 1050 CE. The famous 
poet Vidyapati Thakur (1350–1435 CE) is situated at the 14th generation of 
the Bisphi-Kashyap branch, aligning perfectly with established historical 
datings. 
The system also records key legends associated with political power in the 
region, such as the arrival of Nanyadeva, the founder of the Karnat dynasty. 
According to the Panji texts, Nanyadeva arrived in Mithila on horseback, 
exhausted and thirsty, and tied his horse under a tree. When he attempted to 
cut grass for his horse, a cobra appeared, raising its hood. A Sanskrit 
prophecy was found written nearby, which Nanyadeva could not read. 
Kameshwar Thakur, a learned Brahmin, read the prophecy, revealing that 
Nanyadeva was destined to be the King of Mithila. 
Kameshwar Thakur’s descendant, Chandeshvar Thakkura, later served as the 
minister of war and peace under Harisimhadeva and successfully led a 
military expedition to Nepal, performing the tulā-pūruṣadāna along the 
Vagmati River. When Chandeshvar handed over the state administration to 
Harisinghdev and fled, soldiers pursued him. He threw water on the first 
pursuer, freezing him in place, and threw water on the second pursuer,

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 97 
blinding him, which according to local tradition led to the naming of the 
village Anhra Thadhi. 
The structural subdivisions of the Maithil Brahmin community are 
categorized by the number of Mools associated with each of the primary 
Gotras. 
Gotra | Common Dialectical Pronunciation | Number of Registered Mools 
Sandilya | Sandilyae | 43 
Vatsa | Vatsavaal | 40 
Kashyap | Kashyap | 27 
Savarna | Savarnay | 7 
Katyayana | Katyaayane | 5 
Parashar | Parashari | 7 
Bhardwaj | Bhardwaji | 5 
Garga | Gargay | 3 
These Mools represent the territorial anchors of the community, legitimizing 
the families as indigenous inhabitants of Mithila. For instance, the 
Brahmpura Mool is associated with seven distinct Gotras, including 
Kashyap, Gautam, Vatsa, and Vashistha, while the Karamaha Mool 
comprises both Sandilya and Vatsa Gotra branches, making intermarriage 
within the same Mool possible if the Gotras differ. 
Canonical Prohibitions and the Genetic Rationale of Exogamy 
The primary utility of the Panji Prabandha is to prevent consanguineous 
marriages, thereby avoiding the genetic disorders, developmental delays, and 
immunological vulnerabilities associated with inbreeding. The Panji enforces 
several non-negotiable rules of exclusion before a marriage is certified 
through a Sidhanta issued by the Panjikar. 
In addition to Gotra exogamy, the system enforces Pravara exogamy. For 
example, marriages between individuals of the Vatsa and Savarna Gotras are 
strictly prohibited because they share identical Pravaras, indicating a 
common ritual lineage despite different patrilineal clan designations. The 
system also enforces the Saptapada and Panchapada consanguinity rules, 
which bar couples from sharing a common ancestor within seven generations 
on the paternal side of the groom and five generations on the maternal side of 
the bride.

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 98 
Furthermore, the Kashta Matula rule prevents a man from marrying the 
offspring of his stepmother's brother. The system also prohibits marriages 
between descendants of common paternal or maternal grandfathers. To 
ensure these rules are met, the Panjikars evaluate sixteen distinct maternal 
and paternal bloodlines, known as Kul, of both families. 
Vector Number | Bloodline Vector Evaluated for Sidhanta Approval 
1 | Paternal grandfather of father's paternal grandfather 
2 | Maternal grandfather of father's paternal grandfather 
3 | Paternal grandfather of father's paternal grandmother 
4 | Maternal grandfather of father's paternal grandmother 
5 | Paternal grandfather of father's maternal grandfather 
6 | Maternal grandfather of father's maternal grandfather 
7 | Paternal grandfather of father's maternal grandmother 
8 | Maternal grandfather of father's maternal grandmother 
9 | Paternal grandfather of mother's paternal grandfather 
10 | Maternal grandfather of mother's paternal grandfather 
11 | Paternal grandfather of mother's paternal grandmother 
12 | Maternal grandfather of mother's paternal grandmother 
13 | Paternal grandfather of mother's maternal grandfather 
14 | Maternal grandfather of mother's maternal grandfather 
15 | Paternal grandfather of mother's maternal grandmother 
16 | Maternal grandfather of mother's maternal grandmother 
The structural maintenance of these fragile palm-leaf manuscripts was 
traditionally aligned with the region's climate. The preservation, 
transcriptions, and drying of the manuscripts were conducted during the Leo 
solar cycle, from August 16 to September 16, when the intense heat was 
utilized to dry the leaves and protect them from moisture and pests. 
Social Stratification and the Evolution of the Caste Hierarchy 
Over centuries, the interaction of bloodlines, academic prestige, and marital 
choices led to the creation of five distinct endogamous sub-castes, which 
began to function as independent social strata. The registry originally divided 
families into five qualitative tiers based on the purity of their marriages and 
ancestral accomplishments: Aeyaant, Baaraant, Maddhyamaa, Bhrashtaa, and 
Atibhrashtaa. The top three tiers, comprising thirty-four "Superior Mools,"

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 99 
formed the elite core of the community. Over time, these categories 
crystallized into five socio-ritual ranks. 
Socio-Ritual Class | Local Term | Key Structural and Ritual Characteristics 
Shrotriya | Soit | The highest rank, historically requiring scholarly merit but 
later determined solely by birth and ritual purity. Comprises the top 20 Mools 
of the Aeyaant and Baaraant divisions. 
Yogya | Jog | Formed by Shrotriya families who married into the lower 
Panjeebadh or Maddhyamaa classes, or from three elevated Maddhyamaa 
Mools (Balias-Narsam, Digho-Sannahpur, and Belauche-Sudai). 
Panjeebadh | Bhalmanush | Comprising the fourteen Maddhyamaa Mools. 
They frequently married into the Yogya class to elevate their social standing. 
Vanshadhar | Vanshadhar | Formed by Shrotriyas who fell in rank due to 
marrying outside the thirty-four superior Mools, often receiving a derogatory 
Moolgram suffix. 
Jaiwaar | Jaiwaar | The lowest tier of the classified hierarchy, consisting of 
families who did not maintain rigorous ritual records or married outside the 
classified lineages. 
To manage social mobility within these ranks, the Paainj (or Laukit) system 
was introduced. If a high-ranking family married into a lower-ranking 
family, their social status dropped. However, if their descendants 
subsequently contracted high-status marriages over successive generations, 
they could recover some of their lost rank and were awarded a Paainj—a title 
indicating that they had successfully rehabilitated their status, though they 
were rarely restored to their original high standing. 
The social hierarchy was heavily modified in the nineteenth century by Raja 
Madhav Singh of Darbhanga. In a bid to centralize political authority and 
curb the rising power of rival aristocratic houses, the Raja reconstructed the 
social landscape. He confined the elite Shrotriya class to a specific 
geographic enclave known as Soitpura and severed their social and marital 
interactions with the rest of Maithil society. 
Furthermore, he made it mandatory for any Shrotriya family to obtain his 
personal written permission, known as Parmaangi, before finalizing any 
marriage alliance. This political intervention effectively turned the Shrotriyas 
into dependent landlords under the Darbhanga Raj, converting what was once 
an intellectually active, debating scholarly class into a subservient, isolated 
aristocracy, which ultimately led to the decay of their traditional 
philosophical pursuits.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 100 
The system also preserves records of historical disputes and social 
demotions, such as the case of "Taskar Keshav" (Keshav Jha of Jamasam). 
When Maharaja Lakshmishwar Singh sought to contract a marriage with a 
girl from Keshav's village, Keshav Jha intervened, brought the girl back to 
the village, and forced a passing dye-maker to perform the Kanyadaan. In 
response, the Maharaja summoned the Panjikar and ordered that the title 
"Taskar" (thief) be officially attached to Keshav's lineage, affecting the social 
status of any families associated with them. 
To maintain this complex apparatus of social monitoring, the selection and 
training of Panjikars became a highly professionalized affair. During the 
Darbhanga Raj period, candidates underwent a rigorous ten-year course of 
study in the royal court, mastering genealogy, legal texts, and scriptural 
codes. 
At the end of this training, the candidate had to sit for the Dhaut Pariksha—a 
rigorous public examination overseen by the region's leading scholars. 
Successful candidates were awarded a ceremonial white cloth (Dhoti) and the 
formal title of Panjikar. Following the dissolution of the Darbhanga Raj, this 
formal academic certification ended, and the office of the Panjikar became 
strictly hereditary. 
Archival Classifications of Panji Manuscripts 
The handwritten records preserved by the Panjikars are classified into three 
distinct categories based on their content and purpose 5: 
• 
Gothra Panji: Records detailing the fundamental associations between 
specific Gotras and their primary Mools. 
• 
Patra Panji: Lineage sheets recording the names of children, marital 
alliances, and changes of residence, capturing the historical migrations of 
families across generations. 
• 
Dushan Panji: The registry of faults, documenting structural deviations, 
inter-caste marriages, and other violations of orthodox boundaries. 
Several seminal historical manuscripts are preserved within this archival 
tradition. These include the Mandar Mool manuscript written by Raghudev 
Jha during the late seventeenth and early eighteenth centuries, and the 
subsequent Shakha book composed on palm leaves by his grandson, Panji 
Chhoti (Devanand Jha), between 1760 and 1776 CE.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 101 
Other significant records include the Shakha book of Panji Bhikhia Jha, 
written by Panji Domai Mishra of the Hariamb Mool (a resident of Koilakh), 
and the Shakha book of Panji Harshanand Jha, composed by Panji Jhonti Jha 
(Mitranand Jha). Additionally, the Shakha books of Panji Jhulanand Jha, 
Panji Modanand Jha, and those written between 1925 and 1935 CE in 
Saurath by Panji Lutan Jha and his maternal cousin Panji Nirsu Jha remain 
central reference texts for modern genealogical verification. 
The Subaltern Counter-Archive: The Dushan Panji and Gangesha 
Upadhyaya 
While the official Panji Prabandha presented an idealized image of absolute 
ritual purity and social compliance, the actual practice of Maithil society was 
far more complex. This social reality is preserved in the Dushan Panji, a 
parallel archive that recorded violations of orthodox boundaries. For 
centuries, the Dushan Panji was kept hidden by elite registrars, as its contents 
threatened the high-caste status of several prominent families. 
The Dushan Panji documented structural deviations that the mainstream 
narratives sought to erase, including inter-caste alliances with lower-caste 
individuals, widow remarriages, and illegitimate births. The most significant 
revelation from the Dushan Panji concerns Gangesha Upadhyaya, the 
legendary thirteenth-century founder of the Navya-Nyaya (New Logic) 
school of philosophy and author of the seminal text Tattvachintamani. 
For centuries, elite Maithil historians, such as Ramanath Jha, actively 
suppressed or omitted specific genealogical details of Gangesha's life to 
maintain the narrative of unblemished high-caste lineage. The original 
Dushan Panji manuscripts (such as Record 21//10 of the Chhadan Mool) 
preserve two highly controversial facts 5: 
• 
Paternal Absence: Gangesha was born five years after the death of his 
father ("Pitri Parokshe Panch Varsh Vyatite"), pointing directly to an 
illegitimate or extra-marital conception. 
• 
Inter-Caste Marriage: Gangesha's wife (Vallabha), named Ananda or 
Medha, was a Charmakarini—a woman belonging to the leather-tanning 
(Chamar) caste. 
The preservation of these details in the Dushan Panji reveals that the Panji 
Prabandha did not merely function as an instrument of elite exclusion. 
Rather, it operated as a record of social reality, documenting transgressions

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 102 
with clinical precision and creating a counter-archive that exposes the fluid 
and contested boundaries of historical Maithil society. 
The modern preservation of these records has been marked by plagiarism and 
copyright disputes. For example, the 2009 work Genome Mapping by 
Gajendra Thakur, Nagendra Kumar Jha, and Panjikar Vidyanand Jha was 
reportedly plagiarized in 2010 by Dr. Yoganand Jha (also known as 
Yoganand "Sindhu") under the title Panji Prabandh (Vansh Parichay - First 
Part), with the support of the Shrotriya organization "Maharaja Kameshwar 
Singh Sanskritik Vikas Manch". Similarly, the 1962 work Mithila ki 
Sanskritik Lokchitrakala by painter Laxminath Jha was translated and 
published under the title Aripan in 2008 by Sushila Jha, utilizing scanned 
images from the original book under a grant from the Central Institute of 
Indian Languages, Mysore. 
Cultural Geography and Regional Profiles of Purbanchal Mithila 
The operationalization of the Panji system was historically tied to the cultural 
geography of Mithila. To facilitate marriages, King Harisimhadeva 
established fourteen major regional centers, known as Sabha Gachhis 
(orchard assemblies), where matrimonial negotiations could take place under 
the supervision of the Panjikars. These locations included Saurath, 
Khanagadi, Partapur, Sheohar, Govindpur, Fattepur, Sajhaul, Sukhasana, 
Akhdari, Hemnagar, Balua, Baruali, Samsaul, and Sasaula. Of these, the 
Saurath Sabha (near Madhubani) and the Sasaula Sabha (near Sitamarhi) 
became the most popular. 
The eastern and northern regions of Mithila, encompassing Purnia, Araria, 
and Saharsa (collectively referred to as Purbanchal Mithila), present distinct 
historical and geographical profiles within the Panji records. For example, 
the name of the Araria district is derived from the British administrative 
designation "R. Area" (Rural Area), which was subsequently corrupted into 
the local vernacular. 
Within the Sripur Pargana of Araria, the village of Jahanpur stands as a 
prominent center of Maithil settlement. Originally settled in the early 
sixteenth century by Nikai and Rakai Jha of the Panichobh Jhoua Mool, 
alongside the brothers Haridev and Govind Thakur of the Khandvala Ekma 
Mool in the adjacent village of Satghara, the settlement shifted to Jahanpur in 
the third generation under the leadership of Nikai's grandson, Umanath, and 
Bhajaram Thakur.

Gajendra Thakur — 103 
In 1708 CE, following dreams directing excavation, the village consecrated 
physical idols of Vishnu, Narasimha, Radha-Krishna, and Durga. Jahanpur 
has produced several prominent figures, including Pandit Punyanand Jha, a 
celebrated scholar, writer, and the first postgraduate from the old Purnia 
district, who served as a Member of the Legislative Assembly in Bihar. 
The village was also home to Giranand Thakur, a just zamindar and scholar 
of English and Persian, and Rashmohan Jha, an Honorary Magistrate of the 
Darihara Ratauli Mool. Jahanpur also contributed several freedom fighters to 
the national movement, including Pandit Balabhadra Thakur, Pandit 
Ramsundar Thakur, Pandit Surendra Mohan Thakur, Pandit Gokhulanand 
Thakur, and Pandit Suryamohan Thakur, while its local administration was 
served for decades by Harishchandra Jha (Bechan Jha) of the Sinhouli 
Mandar Mool. 
Other prominent villages in the Sripur Pargana include Chopra Bukhari, 
settled by the Khandvala Behat family who migrated from Tirhut to Katihar, 
and Asura, a highly populated but flood-prone village situated along the 
banks of the Kankai River. 
The Asanga (or Asaja) Pargana comprises several key villages, including 
Begampur, Ekamba, Vishnupur, Asarna, Sakma, Bhawanipur, and Ufraul. 
Among these, Vishnupur is home to families of the Hariamb Ahil and 
Khouade Kurma Mools, including Mitranath Jha, a Persian scholar and 
Tehsildar. The village also claims the lineage of Lal Mishra (also known as 
Doman) of the Hariamb Ahil Mool, whose four sons—Gunanand, 
Tirthanand, Padmanand, and Gajanand—contributed significantly to the 
region. 
Gunanand founded several educational institutions, while Tirthanand's son 
served as a scholar, research authority on the economic history of Purnia, and 
the founder Vice Chancellor of Mithila University. This scholarly tradition 
was continued by his son, Dr. Ratneshwar Mishra, who served as the Head of 
the Post Graduate History Department at Mithila University. 
The Haveli (or Nashira) Pargana was traditionally celebrated for the 
meticulousness of its ritual observances. Shrotriya families resided in this 
region for generations under the protection of the Surgan Loam dynasty, later 
returning to Tirhut during the reign of Raja Madhav Singh after 1760 CE. 
Nashira was also the residence of Jyotirishwar Thakur, the author of the first 
Maithili dictionary and compendium, Varnaratnakara. During periods of

records.text:

Gajendra Thakur — 104 
famine in Bengal, several families migrated to Nashira, including the Ray 
family of the Gangoliwar Digoun Mool, who subsequently settled in Arihana 
in the Katihar district. 
The Dharampur Pargana, spanning the western portions of Purnia, Araria, 
and Katihar, was historically ruled by the Khandwala dynasty and the 
Puhasara rulers of the Surgan Loam Mool. During the reign of the Darbhanga 
ruler Raja Raghav Singh, a local revenue officer named Viru Kurmi 
constructed a fort in Virnagar and ceased paying state revenues. 
A military expedition led by Sarmast Ali Khan, with the assistance of 
Raghav Singh, defeated the rebellion, and the administration of the Nathpur 
and Goradi talukas was subsequently transferred to the Surgan Loam kings of 
Purnia. In western Dharampur, the Janakinagar area was marked by the 
spiritual influence of Mahanth Ramdas, who traveled the region and 
protected local populations from administrative tyranny, a tradition continued 
by his disciple, Mahanth Raghavdas. 
Dharampur also includes the village of Rashadh, a historic center of Sanskrit 
scholarship home to Mahamahopadhyay Harishwar Mishra, and the village 
of Dhamdaha (historically Dharmadiha), which produced scholars such as 
Mahamahopadhyay Chitrapat Jha and Mahamahopadhyay Shyam Sundar 
Thakur. 
Modern Challenges and the Imperative of Digital Archiving 
In the contemporary era, the seven-century-old Panji system faces significant 
operational challenges arising from demographic and socio-economic shifts 
6: 
• 
Geographic Dispersion: The migration of families to metropolitan areas 
and abroad has weakened the localized community networks that 
historically supported the Sabha Gachhis. 
• 
Changing Matrimonial Priorities: Modern marriages increasingly 
prioritize educational background, career compatibility, and personal 
choice over traditional genealogical verification, resulting in nearly 
twenty percent of marriages occurring without Panji verification. 
• 
Economic Unviability: The profession of the Panjikar provides minimal 
financial security in the absence of state or institutional patronage, 
prompting the younger generation of registrar families to pursue 
alternative careers.

• 
Physical Decay of Manuscripts: The primary records, written on fragile 
palm leaves and handmade paper, are highly vulnerable to humidity, 
pests, and decay, raising the risk that centuries of ancestral history may 
be lost. 
To address these challenges, scholars have emphasized the need for 
systematic digitization and archival preservation. Transforming these 
physical manuscripts into a secure, searchable digital database would 
preserve the genealogical heritage of the region, ensuring that its historical 
records and genetic utility remain accessible to future generations.

Executive Summary 
ThePanjiPrabandhis a unique and exhaustive genealogical documentation 
system utilized by the Maithil Brahmin and Karna Kayastha communities of 
Mithila. While its roots trace back to the Vedic period and early medieval era 
(starting circa 450 AD), the system was formally institutionalized and 
standardized 
in1326AD 
(Saka 
1248)under 
the 
patronage 
ofKingHarisimhadevaof the Karnata dynasty.The primary mission of the 
Panji is to maintain "Jati Shuddhi" (purity of lineage) by regulating marriages 
through a complex verification of ancestry. It serves as a dense record of 
Gotras , Mulas (original villages), Shakhas (branches), and titles. The 
system dictates that for a marriage to be valid, the couple must not share a 
common ancestor within specific generational limits (typically seven 
generations on the paternal side and five on the maternal side). This briefing 
outlines the structural framework, historical evolution, and the rigorous 
genealogical investigations required for the issuance of "Siddhanta" 
(marriage eligibility certificates). 
6. Historical Evolution and Codification 
The Maithil Brahmin community evolved from Vedic roots, specifically 
divided 
between 
theYajurvedic(MadhyandinaShakha)andSamavedic(KauthumaShakha)tra
ditions. Over centuries, geographical isolation, climate, and local customs led 
to the distinct identity of "Maithil" Brahmins among the ten categories of 
Indian Brahmins ( Pancha-Gauda and Pancha-Dravida ). 
The Harisimhadeva Reforms (1326 AD) 
In the 14th century, King Harisimhadeva observed a decline in the voluntary 
maintenance of genealogical records ( Samuha-Lekhya ). To prevent

accidental prohibited marriages (incestuous by Vedic standards), he 
established an official state department for genealogy. 
• 
First Panjikars: The King appointed Gunakar Jha (for Brahmins), 
Shankardatta (for Karna Kayasthas), and Vijaydatta (for Kshatriyas) to 
collect and codify all existing family records. 
• 
Incentive for Accuracy: The system was built on "Absolute Source 
Fidelity," where Panjikars traveled from village to village, interviewing 
family heads to record their Bijipurush (founding ancestor) and Mula 
Gram (original village). 
7. Structural Framework: Gotra, Mula, and Pravara 
The Panji system classifies families based on three primary markers: 
I. Gotras (Lineage from Sages) 
The Maithil Brahmin community is divided into20Gotras . Seven of these are 
considered the most prominent and populous.| Major Gotras (7) | Minor 
Gotras (13) || ------ | ------ || Shandilya, Vatsa, Kashyapa, Savarna, Parashara, 
Bharadwaj, Katyayana | Garga, Kaushika, Alambukaksha, Krishnatreya, 
Gautama,

II. Mulas (Roots) and Mula-Grams 
There are167recognizedMulasin Mithila, derived from the original village of 
the founding ancestor ( Bijipurush ). 
• 
From these 167, 34 to 35 "Mukhya" (Main) Mulas were identified as 
primary ancestral nodes. 
• 
The Panji identifies the current residence of a family as their Shakha 
(branch), but their identity remains tied to the Mula . 
III. Pravaras 
Pravaras refer to the ancestral sages who authored Vedic hymns. Maithil 
Brahmins are classified into: 
• 
Tri-Pravara: Descendants of three specific sages. 
• 
Pancha-Pravara: Descendants of five specific sages (e.g., Vatsa, 
Savarna, and Garga gotras). 
8. Social Stratification and Categories 
The system categorizes families into hierarchical ranks based on their 
adherence to rituals and historical conduct:

• 
Shrotriya: The highest rank, subdivided into eight grades. They observe 
the strictest marriage rules (discarding 7 paternal and 5 maternal 
generations). 
• 
Yogya: The middle tier, subdivided into thirteen grades. 
• 
Panjibaddha (Vanshaja): The baseline rank of recorded families. 
• 
Jayavar: Those who were not originally included in the formal Panji 
records but are part of the broader community. 
9. The Mechanics of Marriage Eligibility 
The central function of the Panji is to determine if a prospective bride and 
groom are"Asvajan"(unrelated within prohibited degrees). 
The Sapinda Rule 
Marriage is prohibited if the couple shares a common ancestor within: 
• 
Paternal Side: 6 or 7 generations. 
• 
Maternal Side: 5 generations. 
The "Chhapthi" Investigation (Six-Point Verification) 
A Panjikar (Genealogist) must verify 32 points of ancestry (the Utedh ) for 
both parties. This includes the ancestry of: 
• 
Paternal and Maternal Grandfathers/Grandmothers. 
• 
Great-grandparents and beyond. 
• 
The investigation ensures that none of the 16 "Chhapthi" (critical 
genealogical nodes) of the bride appear in the groom's paternal lineage. 
The Siddhanta Process 
Before any marriage can take place, the Panjikar must issue aSiddhanta. 
This is a written document certifying that the union is "Shastric" (scripturally 
valid). Without a Siddhanta, a marriage is considered "Ashastriya" (invalid) 
and the offspring may be socially marginalized. 
10. Historical Records and Anomalies 
The Panji is more than a marriage registry; it is a historical archive. The 
documents record various sociological data points: 
• 
Academic Titles: Extensive records of titles like Mahamahopadhyaya , 
Upadhyaya , and Pandit from as early as 450 AD. 
• 
Historical Figures: The records trace the lineage of notable figures such 
as the poet Vidyapati , the philosopher Gangesha Upadhyaya (author of

Tattva Chintamani ), and even the connections of Chaitanya 
Mahaprabhu (whose teacher was from the Karamha Mula). 
• 
Migration and "Dosha": The Panji records "Doshas" (faults or deviations 
in lineage), such as marriages into unknown castes or prohibited families, 
ensuring that the social standing of a lineage is transparent. 
• 
Script Preservation: The Panji records are traditionally maintained in the 
Tirhuta (Maithili) script. The survival of this script is intrinsically linked 
to the Panji Prabandh. 
11. Current Challenges and Maintenance 
• 
Script Decay: As the knowledge of the Tirhuta script declines, fewer 
people can read the original palm-leaf ( Tal-Patra ) records, leading to a 
reliance on Devanagari transliterations. 
• 
Preservation of Records: The ancient palm-leaf manuscripts are fragile. 
Traditional preservation involves drying them in the sun during the 
month when the Sun enters the Leo zodiac sign (August/September). 
• 
Role of the Panjikar: The Panjikar is viewed as the "Protector of 
Culture." The document notes a modern decline in the ethical standards 
of some practitioners and emphasizes the need for a new generation of 
trained genealogists to maintain the system's integrity. 
Conclusion:The Panji Prabandh represents an unparalleled genealogical 
achievement, maintaining a continuous record of a population for over 1, 
years. It remains a critical instrument for social organization and cultural 
identity in Mithila, functioning as a "Genome Map" that predates modern 
genetic science by centuries. 
Panji Prabandh: A Comprehensive Study Guide to Maithil Genealogy 
This study guide provides a detailed synthesis of the "Panji Prabandh," a 
unique genealogical system practiced primarily by the Maithil Brahmins, 
Karna Kayasthas, and Kshatriyas of the Mithila region. Spanning from 
approximately 450 AD to 2009 AD, this system serves as a traditional form 
of genome mapping and lineage management.

The 1,500-Year-Old Social Algorithm: Decoding Mithila’s Ancient 
"Genome Map" 
In the modern age, we view services like 23andMe or Ancestry.com as the 
pinnacle of genealogical tech. Yet, these are fundamentallyreactivetools—
they tell us what we are based on the past. In the Mithila region of South 
Asia, an extraordinary "bio-social ledger" known as thePanjiPrabandhhas 
operated for over 1, years as aproactive biological firewall. Established as 
early as 450 AD and codified into a decentralized social contract by King 
Harisimhadeva in 1326 AD (Shaka 1248), this system didn't just record 
ancestry; it was designed to predictively determine the biological viability of 
the next generation. It is, in every sense, a distributed human database that 
put data integrity above social ego. 
12. The 32-Point Pedigree Check (The "Original Algorithm")

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 114 
Long before the advent of digital nodes, the Mithila system utilized a 
rigorous concept calledUteghto verify lineage. To determine if a union was 
permissible, a genealogist had to identify theBattisKula (32 lineages). This 
required tracking 32 specific ancestral nodes for every individual—including 
paternal and maternal grandparents and great-grandparents across multiple 
intersecting lines. In a pre-paper society, this represented a massive 
computational challenge. The Panjikar (genealogist) had to mentally 
"query" these 32 nodes for every proposed union, a non-linear verification 
process that ensured the "database" remained free of redundant genetic loops. 
13. The "Siddhanta" — Marriage by Permission 
The architect of the modern Panji system,KingHarisimhadeva, formalized 
the role of the Panjikar as the ultimate gatekeeper of social data. No 
marriage 
was 
considered 
valid 
or 
"shastric" 
without 
a 
mandatorySiddhanta—a formal certificate of permission. This certificate 
was a declaration ofAsvajanatva(non-relatedness), proving the couple were 
not "Swajana" (closely related)."The Panji-Prabandh's primary rule became 
that without a 'Siddhanta,' a marriage would be considered un-shastric 
(unauthorized)." 
14. Ancient Genetic Safeguards (The 6/7 Generation Rule) 
The system’s "source code" was theSapindalogic, which translates to the 
"sharing of body particles"—the ancient equivalent of shared genetic 
markers. To ensure maximum genetic distance (or heterosis) and prevent the 
degradation 
caused 
by 
inbreeding, 
the 
Panji 
enforced 
a 
specificasymmetricalfiltering system: 
• 
Paternal Side: Marriage was prohibited within seven generations on the 
father’s side. 
• 
Mother’s Side: Marriage was prohibited within five generations on the 
mother’s side.By mapping these generations with such granularity, the 
Mithila society created a manual genome map that identified potential 
"genetic overlaps" centuries before the double helix was ever visualized. 
15. The Honesty of "Dosh" (Blemish) Records 
While most family trees are edited to hide "scandals," the Panji Prabandh 
functioned on the principle of absolutedataintegrity. The records contain 
transparent entries regardingDosh , or lineage blemishes. For the "social 
algorithm" to remain functional, negative data was considered just as 
valuable as positive data. Erasing a blemish was seen as corrupting the

database, which would cause the entire genealogical map to fail. The source 
records include startlingly honest data points, such as marriages with 
"unknown castes," "devadasis," or even specific mentions of "Yavani" 
(Muslim) connections—such as the record of Daulti Jahan —and "Chamar" 
(leather worker) lineages. 
16. Materiality and Preservation: The "Palm-Leaf Server" 
The preservation of this 1,500-year history was a masterpiece of ancient 
knowledge systems. The records were the lifeblood of theTirhutascript, 
written onTala Patra(palm leaves) using an indelible ink derived from forest 
herbs. Because these physical "servers" were susceptible to decay, the 
Panjikars followed a strict maintenance protocol: the leaves had to be dried 
in the sun duringSinghaRashi (when the sun is in Leo, typically August 16 
to September 15), the period of the year when solar intensity is strongest. 
This prevented moisture and fungal rot, ensuring the data survived for over a 
millennium without state patronage. 
Conclusion: A Legacy of Memory 
The Panji Prabandh is more than a relic; it is a sophisticated bio-social ledger 
that has survived through the sheer social will to maintain data accuracy. 
Today, as we transition these palm leaves into digital and Devanagari 
formats, we are essentially migrating a 1,500-year-old human database into a 
new architecture.In our age of temporary digital footprints and "deleted" 
histories, what will remain of our lineage 1, years from now? 
Strategic Framework for the Digitization and Modernization of Mithila’s 
Panji Records 
17. Executive Context: The Living Genome (450 AD – Present) 
The Mithila Panji system represents a monumental "Genome Mapping"

The 'Panji Prabandh' system constitutes the world’s most extensive "Genome 
Mapping" apparatus, preserving the biological and spiritual integrity of the 
Maithil Brahmin community from approximately 450 AD to the present. This 
institutionalized genealogical record is designed to maintain Jati Shuddhi 
(caste purity) and prevent Sapinda (consanguineous) unions. By 
meticulously tracking the "Seed" of each lineage, the system ensures that 
every marital alliance complies with the rigors of Shastric law.Historically, 
the transition from decentralized memory to formalized documentation was 
mandated in 1326 AD (Saka 1248) by King Harisimhadeva. Disturbed by the 
accidental violation of marriage rules among scholars—most notably the case 
of Mahapandit Harinath Upadhyaya—the King centralized genealogical 
authority. He entrusted the 'Panji Prabandh' toGunakarJha of the Mandar 
Moola (Phinandah branch), appointing him the primary custodian of these 
records. While the system was initially intended for Brahmins, Kshatriyas, 
and Kayasthas, it has survived in its most rigorous form among Maithil 
Brahmins and Karna Kayasthas. Modern census data within the system 
confirms the existence of185totalPanjis: 32 designated for the Srotriya tier 
and 153 for the Yogya and Panjibadh (Vanshaj) tiers. 
30. Core Taxonomic Classifications: Gotra, Pravara, and Moola 
Genealogical verification relies on a tripartite taxonomy that defines an 
individual’s identity: Gotra (lineage), Pravara (illustrious sages), and Moola 
(ancestral village). These classifications act as the primary filter for 
eligibility. 
The 20 Gotras and the Seven Foundations 
The Maithil Brahmin community is organized into twenty Gotras. Of these, 
seven are recognized as the "Main Gotras" which form the bedrock of the 
community: 
• 
Shandilya

Pravara and Ritual Compatibility 
The Pravara reflects the specific Rigvedic hymn-composers within a Gotra. 
Lineages are categorized by the number of these ancestors: 
• 
Pancha-Pravara (Five Sages): Exclusively the Vatsa, Savarna, and 
Garga Gotras. 
• 
Tri-Pravara (Three Sages): The remaining seventeen Gotras (e.g., 
Shandilya, Kashyapa, Parashara).Critical Prohibition: A specific 
technical barrier exists between the Vatsa and Savarna Gotras. 
Marriage between them is strictly forbidden as they share identical 
ancestral roots— Aurva, Chyavana, Bhargava, Jamadagnya, and 
Apnuvana —rendering them a single biological unit for the purposes of 
Sapinda restrictions. 
The Moola Hierarchy 
There are167total Moolas(Ancestral Villages) identified, with35 Main 
Moolas serving as the primary markers of social standing: 
• 
Ayanti Shrestha (First Class): Khandauriya, Khauadiya, Budhabariya, 
Madariya, Darihariya, Ghausautiya, Pisaubtiya, Karmaha, Narauniya, 
Babhaniyamaya, Hariambamaya, Sarisabiya, and Sodarpuriye. 
• 
Dwitiya Shreni (Second Class): Gangolivar, Pautivar, Kujaulivar, 
Alevar, Pahidvar, Sakadivar, Palivar, Bisbevar, Panevar, Upachitivar, 
Tandulivar, Kataivar, and Tilaitivar. 
• 
Madhyam Moola (Middle Class): Dirghghe, Baleunchai, Ekahare, 
Panchobhe, Baliyase, Jajival, Takival, and Mandue (including the 
Khanama branch of Phinandah). 
31. The Uteh Framework: The Rule of 32 Ancestors 
The 'Uteh' is the formal ancestral introduction ( parichay ) required to 
confirm that a union is not Sapinda . The Panjikar must verify32Moolas 
representing 32 ancestors—16 from the paternal side and 16 from the 
maternal side.The standard for "generational abandonment" (ensuring no 
common ancestor exists within a specific degree) is rigorous: 
• 
Srotriya: Must abandon 7 generations on the paternal side and 5 
generations on the maternal. 
• 
Panjibadh/Yogya: Must abandon 6 generations on the paternal side and 
5 generations on the maternal.While historical texts like Manusmriti 
suggest 7 paternal generations, the influence of scholars like Mahesh

The violation of Sapinda or Svajana (kinship) rules results in the union 
being declared Ashastric . Progeny from such unions were traditionally 
classified as 'Chandala' and excluded from the honors of Aryatva . 
The Five Absolute Prohibitions

• 
Non-equality of Gotra: Parties must not share a Gotra.

The Dushun (Taint) Records 
The system maintains the 'Dushun Panji', a register of historical lineage

The administrative culmination of the Panjikar’s work is the issuance of 
theSiddhanta, also known as theAsvajana-patra. This document serves as 
the professional certification that a union is scripturally and customarily 
valid. 
Administrative Mandates 
The Panjikar must strictly adhere to the following: 
• 
Cross-Referencing: Validating the Sakha Panji (branch) against the 
Moola Panji (original root). 
•

Laukik ) remains untainted. 
•

Panjikar must follow the ancient directive: "Jalat Raksha, Tailat Raksha, 
Raksha Sthula Bandhanat" (Protect from water, protect from oil, protect 
from loose binding).No marriage within the Maithil Brahmin community 
is considered valid or socially recognized without the formalized 
Asvajana-patra . The Panjikar stands not as a clerk, but as the Protector

41. Conclusion: A Living History 
The *PanjiPrabandh* is a unique cultural treasure, a system of human 
record-keeping that predates modern genetics by nearly a thousand years. It 
was born out of a defensive necessity to preserve a distinct identity during a 
period of shifting social landscapes.By mastering these categories—Gotra, 
Moola, and Pravara—you are not merely learning about a social hierarchy; 
you are learning to read the map of a civilization. This system ensures that 
every Maithil is not an isolated individual, but a vital link in a historical 
chain stretching back to the dawn of the Vedic era. 
TheChhaphiSystem: A Procedural Guide toMaithilGenealogical Logic 
As a Senior Ethnologist specializing in the preservation of Vedic social 
structures, I present this guide to thePanjisystem. This ancient Maithil 
Brahmin record-keeping method is one of the most sophisticated 
examples of historical information architecture in the world. Often 
described as a precursor to modern genome mapping, the system uses a 
mathematical logic calledChhaphi to prevent prohibited marriages and 
ensure lineage purity. 
42. Foundations of the Panji System 
The Panji system was formally institutionalized in Shaka 1248 (1326 AD) 
under the patronage ofKingHarisimhadeva, the Karnat Dynasty ruler of 
Mithila. The system arose as a response to a critical genealogical crisis: noble 
families were inadvertently entering into Svajana (incestuous or prohibited) 
unions because oral traditions had become fragmented. To address this, the 
King commissioned the scholarGunakarJha to document and consolidate 
the lineages of all Maithil Brahmin families.The primary objective was to 
establish a permanent, written "Human Database" to ensure every marriage 
adhered to strict Vedic requirements. By centralizing these records, the 
Mithila state ensured that couples were Asvajana (not related within 
prohibited degrees), thereby preventing the birth of offspring who would 
suffer a loss of social and religious standing—a status referred to in Smriti 
law as Chandala .While the logic of the Panji remains universal across the

45. The Rules of Marriage Eligibility (Sidhanta) 
Once the Chhaphi is calculated, the Panjikar must verify several other 
prohibitions before issuing a final verdict, known asSidhanta. 
Eligibility Checklist: 
• 
Exogamy of Gotra and Pravara: The bride and groom cannot belong to 
the same Gotra or share the same Pravara (noting the Vatsa/Savarna 
shared lineage rule). 
• 
Sapinda Avoidance (Paternal): Historically 7 generations, but modified 
by scholars like MM Mahesh Thakur ; the 6th generation is currently 
treated as the limit for eligibility. 
• 
Sapinda Avoidance (Maternal): The couple must not be blood relatives 
within 5 generations. 
• 
Step-Maternal Uncle Rule ( Katha Mama ): A man cannot marry the 
child of his step-mother’s brother. 
• 
Asvajana Status: The Panjikar must confirm the couple are Asvajana 
(unrelated within these bounds).The ultimate goal of these rules is to 
ensure the marriage is Shastric . Unions failing these checks result in 
Svajana offspring, who are historically compared to the "Chandala" 
class, effectively losing their religious status and continuity. 
46. The Panjikar: The Human Database 
ThePanjikaris the professional custodian of theSamhu-Lekhya(collective 
records). These records were traditionally preserved onTalapatra (palm 
leaves) using a specialized ink made from forest herbs ( Khadahi ), designed 
to be resistant to water and oil. Preservation is so vital that the records are 
traditionally sun-dried only when the Sun is in Leo (mid-August to mid-
September) to ensure maximum protection against moisture. 
Primary Materials of the Panjikar: 
• 
Mula Panji: Foundational records of ancestral villages and original 
sources. 
• 
Sakha Panji: Branch records tracking how families migrated to different 
villages. 
• 
Utegh: The technical list of 32 ancestors used to calculate the 16 
Chhaphi nodes.

(450 AD – 2009 AD) 
48. Introduction: The "Family GPS" of Mithila (Student Edition) 
For over a millennium, the Maithil society of North Bihar and Nepal has 
maintained thePanjiPrabandh, one of the most sophisticated genealogical 
systems in human history. Imagine a massive, 2,000-page "family map" or 
database that tracks every ancestor, original village, and marriage for nearly 
1, years. This system functions like a strategic "Family GPS," preserving the 
identity and biological integrity of the society across generations. While the 
roots of these records trace back to 450 AD, the system was formally 
standardized in 1326 AD (Saka 1248) underKingHarisimhaDeva.The 
catalyst for this formalization was a famous "identity crisis" involving 
the great scholarPandit Harinath Upadhyaya . Despite his immense 
knowledge of the scriptures, he accidentally married a close cousin (his

maternal lineage. When he realized his error, the society was shocked. 
Acting like a modern-day CEO ordering a massive "system update," King 
Harisimha Deva decreed that all family trees must be documented in 
writing—moving from Smriti (memory) to Lekhya (written records). This 
created a biological and social audit system, ensuring that people did not 
marry close relatives (protecting genetic health) and providing every Maithil 
Brahman with a verifiable "identity code."SoWhat?: The Panji Prabandh was 
a precursor to modern genetic mapping. Centuries before digital databases or 
DNA testing, Mithila established a rigorous social audit that prioritized 
genetic diversity and social accountability, creating one of the most stable 
and documented social structures in the world. 
49. The Building Blocks: Gotra, Mool, and Pravara 
To navigate the Panji, one must understand the technical architecture used to 
define an individual’s identity. Every Maithil Brahman is identified by a 
unique combination of three pillars: 
• 
The 20 Gotras (Clans): These represent lineages originating from 
specific Vedic Sages. As per the source (Page 0.), the 20 Gotras are: 1. 
Shandilya, 2. Vatsa, 3. Savarna, 4. Kashyap, 5. Parashar, 6. Bharadwaj, 
7. Katyayan, 8. Garg, 9. Kaushik, 10. Alambyukaksh, 11. Krishnatrey, 
12. Gautam, 13. Mudgalya, 14. Vashistha, 15. Kaundilya, 16. 
Upamanyu, 17. Kapil, 18. Vishnuvriddhi, 19. Tandee, and 20. Jatukarna. 
• 
Mool (The Root Village): This defines the original village where a 
family branch started. There are 167 total Mools , but 35 are recognized 
as "Main Mools." Historically, there were 34, but the Phanandah Mool 
(Khanama branch) was added as the 35th due to its rising status. Mools 
are categorized into three social grades: 
• 
Ayandishrestha (Highest): Includes villages like Kharaure, Mandar, 
Sarisab, and Sodorpur . 
• 
Dwitiya (Second): Includes Gangoliwar, Kujoliwar, and Pailwar . 
• 
Madhyam (Medium): Includes Dighway, Belonche, and Ichhare . 
• 
Pravara (The Sages): Families are classified based on the number of 
sages who composed Vedic hymns in their lineage: Tri-pravara (3 
sages) or Pancha-pravara (5 sages).

The "Beeji Purush": This is the "Seed Ancestor"—the earliest known 
forefather who established a specific family line (e.g., Pinayan Bhatt,

written. This prevented the "erasure of history" and ensured that every 
individual was anchored to a specific historical and geographical point. 
50. The Marriage Algorithm: Rules of Adhikar (Eligibility) 
The Panji system is essentially a complex algorithm used to verify marriage 
eligibility ( Adhikar ). The process is legalistic and scientifically rigorous. 
• 
The Generational Rule: To ensure genetic distance, the system enforces 
the Sapinda rule. For Shrotriya (highest class) families, one must avoid 
7 generations on the father's side and 5 generations on the mother's 
side. For Panjibaddh families, the rule is 6 generations on the father's 
side and 5 on the mother's. 
• 
The "Chhathi" (The Sixteen Branches): A Panjikar must cross-check 16 
specific ancestral relations to ensure no shared bloodline. These include: 
• 
Paternal grandfather’s paternal grandfather. 
• 
Paternal grandfather’s maternal grandfather. 
• 
Paternal grandmother’s paternal grandfather. 
• 
Paternal grandmother’s maternal grandfather. ...and so on, covering 16 
distinct maternal and paternal paths (Source Page 0.9-0.). 
• 
The "Siddhanta-patra": Once eligibility is proven, the Panjikar issues a 
Siddhanta-patra (or Asvajanat ), a marriage certificate confirming the 
couple is not Svajana (too closely related). 
• 
Utedh (Pedigree Analysis): This involves the exhaustive tracking of 32 
maternal and paternal lines (Mools) of both the bride and groom to 
ensure total genealogical distance.So What? : This process functions as a 
"social audit." By prioritizing scientific genetic distance over personal 
choice, Mithila maintained high biological health and social consistency 
for centuries.
